layout_blocks.text: Mixed media collage on canvas with Shou Sugi Ban frame

In 1898, the newly-appointed USPS Postmaster of Lake City, South Carolina, Frazier B. Baker and his infant daughter, Julia Bruce, were lynched in a violent opposition to Frazier's appointment. In 1901, Frazier's wife, Laura, and their four remaining children were referred to the post office for residency.

There are no known images of Frazier B. Baker. Artist Stan Squirewell created Baker: a mixed media collage, where he has envisioned Frazier and added him to the famous photo of his family. While violence stripped Frazier of his life and his legacy, Squirewell works to reimagine Frazier's presence at the post office and provider and reinserts him with his family.

classify.rationale: The image depicts a historical group portrait featuring multiple individuals, indicative of a formal photographic style.

basic_description.description: The image depicts a group of nine individuals, predominantly children, arranged in two rows with a person standing at the back. They are dressed in vibrant, multi-patterned attire featuring various textures and fabrics, including checks and floral patterns. The overall condition of the photograph appears worn, with some faded areas, and it is framed in a wooden frame with a slightly distressed finish.
